zh.Prestbury, Cheshire East district, Chesh: 英國英格蘭爾普雷斯特波利
zh.lifeSummary: 當William de Rode於1230年出生在英國爾諾斯 洛德時，他的父親Thomas de Rode47歲，母親Cecily Jordan41歲。他於1249年與Eva Watton在英國英格蘭爾結婚。他們至少是1個兒子和3個女兒的父母。他去世於1304年在英國英格蘭爾切斯特享年74歲。

zh-hans.Prestbury, Cheshire East district, Chesh: 英国英格兰爾普雷斯特波利
zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当William de Rode于1230年出生在英国爾諾斯 洛德时，他的父亲Thomas de Rode47岁，母亲Cecily Jordan41岁。他于1249年与Eva Watton在英国英格兰爾结婚。他们至少是1个儿子和3个女儿的父母。他去世于1304年在英国英格兰爾切斯特享年74岁。

nameMeanings.name: Rode
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Otto, Erwin, Ewald, Hans, Heinz, Oskar, Viktor, Claus, Frieda, Fritz, Gerhard, Horst.
German and French: from a short form of any of various ancient Germanic personal names with the first element hrōd ‘renown’. Compare Robert , Rudiger .

North German, Danish, and English: topographic name for someone who lived on land cleared for cultivation or in a clearing in woodland, from Middle Low German rode, Danish rothe, Old English rod. There are a number of places in England named with this word, for example Rode in Cheshire, and the surname may derive from any of these. Compare Rhode .

variants.name: Ruth
meanings: German: from a short form of any of several ancient Germanic personal names formed with hrōd ‘renown’ (see Rode ).
(Rüth): habitational name from a place so named near Euskirchen in Rhineland.

English: from Middle English reuth(e) ‘pity’, a nickname perhaps for a charitable person or for a pitiable one. The personal name Ruth was little used in England in the Middle Ages among non-Jews, and is unlikely to have had any influence on the surname.

variants.name: Rody
meanings: German: variant of   Rode   1.
Scottish: variant of Rodie, from a pet form of Roderick .

variants.name: Robert
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Marcel, Gilles, Normand, Armand, Lucien, Pierre, Emile, Michel, Edouard, Gaston, Yves.

French, Walloon, English, German, Dutch, Flemish, and Breton (of French origin); Hungarian (Róbert): from the ancient Germanic personal name Ro(d)bert (Middle English Robert, Old French Ro(d)bert), composed of the elements hrōd ‘fame, renown’ + berht ‘bright, famous’. This is found occasionally in Anglo-Saxon England before the Conquest, but in the main it was introduced into England by the Normans and quickly became popular among all classes of society. Pet forms and diminutives are found from an early date, Robin being particularly common. Compare Robair , Robar , Robare , and Rober .
In some cases also an Americanized form of one or more similar (like-sounding) Jewish surnames.

variants.name: Rodeman
meanings: Americanized form of German Rodemann:
topographic name for someone who lived on woodland cleared for cultivation, a variant of Rode 2 with the addition of Middle Low German man ‘man’.
from an ancient Germanic personal name, Hrodman, formed with hrōd ‘renown, fame’ + man ‘man’.

variants.name: Winterroth
meanings: German: topographic name for someone who lived on the north side of a clearing (see   Rode  ).

variants.name: Roth
meanings: German and Jewish (Ashkenazic): nickname for a person with red hair, from Middle High German rōt, German rot ‘red’. As a Jewish name it is at least in part artificial: its frequency as a Jewish surname is disproportionate to the number of Jews who, one may reasonably assume, were red-headed during the period of surname adoption. This form of the German surname (especially in this sense and in the sense 2 below) is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine), where it is most common, and in some other European countries, e.g. Czechia and Croatia.

German and English (Middlesex): topographic name for someone who lived on land that had been cleared, from Old High German rod, Middle English roth(e) (Old English roth) ‘clearing’. In England, the name may also be a habitational name from any of the places like Rothend in Ashdon (Essex), Roe End in Markyate (Hertfordshire), Roe Green in Hatfield (Hertfordshire), or Roe Green in Sandon (Hertfordshire).
German: from a short form of any of various ancient Germanic personal names with the first element hrōd ‘renown’. Compare Rode 1, Ross 4.

variants.name: Rohde
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erwin, Kurt, Otto, Bernd, Hans, Heino, Heinz, Ewald, Gerhard, Helmut, Hermann, Klaus.
German and Danish: variant of   Rode   2 and a habitational name from any of various places called Rohde or Rohden in Lower Saxony, Saxony, Westphalia, and Hesse. Compare   Rhode   and   Rhody  .

variants.name: Roady
meanings: Perhaps an Americanized form of German   Rode  .

variants.name: Rodich
meanings: Americanized form of Serbian and Croatian Rodić: patronymic from a short form of a Slavic compound personal name based on the element rod ‘family, relatives’, e.g. Rodoslav.
Americanized form of Slovenian Rodič: surname probably related to (or derived from) Rode . Alternatively, it may be of the same origin as the Serbian and Croatian name above.
German: perhaps a variant of Rohdich, from a personal name based on ancient Germanic hrōd ‘renown, victory’.

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of the various boys' names with the first syllable Ed-, especially Edward .

variants.name: Edward
meanings: From an Old English personal name derived from ēad ‘prosperity, riches’ + weard ‘guard’. This has been one of the most successful of all Old English names, in frequent use from before the Conquest to the present day, and even being exported into other European languages. It was the name of three Anglo-Saxon kings and has been borne by eight kings of England since the Norman Conquest. It is also the name of the youngest son of Queen Elizabeth II . The most influential early bearer was King Edward the Confessor ( ?1002–66 ; ruled 1042–66 ). In a troubled period of English history, he contrived to rule fairly and (for a time at any rate) firmly. But in the latter part of his reign he paid more attention to his religion than to his kingdom. He died childless, and his death sparked off conflicting claims to his throne, which were resolved by the victory of William the Conqueror at the Battle of Hastings. His memory was honoured by Normans and English alike, for his fairness and his piety. Edward's mother was Norman; he had spent part of his youth in Normandy; and William claimed to have been nominated by Edward as his successor. Edward was canonized in the 12th century, and came to be venerated throughout Europe as a model of a Christian king.

historicEvents.title: 100 Years of Conflict Between England and France Ends
historicEvents.description: Treaty of Paris was signed on December 4, 1259, which ended 100 years of conflict between England and France.

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

en.lifeSummary: When William de Rode was born in 1230, in North Rode, Cheshire, England, his father, Thomas de Rode, was 47 and his mother, Cecily Jordan, was 41. He married Eva Watton in 1249, in Cheshire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 son and 3 daughters. He died in 1304, in Chester, Cheshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 74.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque William de Rode est né en 1230, à North Rode, Cheshire, Angleterre, son père, Thomas de Rode, avait 47 ans et sa mère, Cecily Jordan, avait 41 ans. Il a épousé Eva Watton en 1249, à Cheshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils et 3 filles. Il est décédé en 1304, à Chester, Cheshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 74 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Als William de Rode 1230 in North Rode, Cheshire, England geboren wurde, war sein Vater Thomas de Rode 47 und seine Mutter Cecily Jordan 41. Er hat 1249 in Cheshire, England, United Kingdom Eva Watton geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 1 Sohn und 3 Töchter. Er starb 1304 im Alter von 74 Jahren in Chester, Cheshire, England, United Kingdom.

it.lifeSummary: Quando William de Rode è nato il nel 1230 nel North Rode, Cheshire, England, il suo padre, Thomas de Rode, aveva 47 anni e il suo madre, Cecily Jordan, aveva 41 anni. Lui sposato Eva Watton nel 1249, nel Cheshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Erano genitori di almeno 1 figlio e 3 figlie. Morì nel 1304, nel Chester, Cheshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 74 anni.

ko.lifeSummary: William de Rode(이)가 1230년 영국 체실르 노르스 로드에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Thomas de Rode(은)는 47세였고, 그의 어머니 Cecily Jordan(은)는 41세였다. 그는 1249년 영국 잉글랜드 체실르에서 Eva Watton(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들과 세 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1304년 영국 잉글랜드 체실르 체스터에서 74세의 나이로 사망했다.

Он женился на женщине по имени Eva Watton в 1249 г. в местности чешир, Англия, Великобритания. У них был по крайней мере 1 сын и 3 дочери. Он умер в 1304 г. в местности честир, чешир, Англия, Великобритания в возрасте 74 лет.
